curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 6 (PM Pathway), Learning Module 4: Liability-Driven and Index-Based Strategies

drills.los_text: evaluate strategies for managing a single liability

questions.stem: *Immunization* *most likely* is:

options.A: the process of structuring and managing a fixed-income bond portfolio to minimize the variance in the realized rate of return over a known time horizon.
options.B: Immunization is the practice of maximizing the expected return of a bond portfolio without regard to the timing of any obligations.
options.C: Immunization refers to eliminating credit risk by holding only government-guaranteed securities.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the process of structuring and managing a fixed-income bond portfolio to minimize the variance in the realized rate of return over a known time horizon; this variance arises from the volatility of future interest rates; default risk is neglected because portfolio bonds are assumed to have default probabilities approaching zero.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Immunization is the process of structuring and managing a fixed-income bond portfolio to minimize the variance in the realized rate of return over a known time horizon. This variance arises from the volatility of future interest rates. Default risk is neglected at this point because the portfolio bonds are assumed to have default probabilities that approach zero.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Immunization targets a KNOWN horizon obligation and minimizes return variance to it — return maximization is not the objective.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The technique addresses INTEREST RATE risk over a known horizon, not credit risk elimination.

questions.stem: The *most obvious way to immunize a single liability* *most likely* is:

options.A: no method.
options.B: buy a zero-coupon bond that matures on the obligation's due date.
options.C: no matching.

rationales.A: Zero-coupon.
rationales.B:
Full statement: buy a zero-coupon bond that matures on the obligation's due date; bond's face value matches liability amount; no cash flow reinvestment risk (no coupons); no price risk (held to maturity); any interest rate volatility over bond's lifetime irrelevant; problem is that in many financial markets, zero-coupon bonds are not available; perfect immunization by zero-coupon bond sets standard to measure performance of coupon-bearing bond strategies.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The most obvious way to immunize the interest rate risk on a single liability is to buy a zero-coupon bond that matures on the obligation's due date. The bond's face value matches the liability amount. There is no cash flow reinvestment risk because there are no coupon payments to reinvest, and there is no price risk because the bond is held to maturity. Any interest rate volatility over the bond's lifetime is irrelevant in terms of the asset's ability to pay off the liability. The problem is that in many financial markets, zero-coupon bonds are not available. Nevertheless, the perfect immunization provided by a zero-coupon bond sets a standard to measure the performance of immunizing strategies using coupon-bearing bonds.'
rationales.C: Zero-coupon match.

questions.stem: *Coupon bond immunization via Macaulay duration matching* *most likely*:

options.A: no relationship.
options.B: no immunization.
options.C: at the Macaulay duration point in time, price effect and coupon reinvestment effect cancel each other out.

rationales.A: Duration matching.
rationales.B: MacDur = horizon.
rationales.C:
Full statement: at the Macaulay duration point in time, price effect and coupon reinvestment effect cancel each other out; therefore investor having investment horizon equal to bond's Macaulay duration is effectively protected (immunized) from interest rate risk (price and coupon reinvestment effects offset for either higher or lower rates); rising rates → bond price falls (measured by money duration) but future value of reinvested coupons goes up.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Assume that the bond is currently priced at par value. Then, an instantaneous, one-time, upward (parallel) shift occurs in the yield curve. The bond's value falls. That drop in value is estimated by the money duration of the bond. Recall that the money duration is the bond's modified duration statistic multiplied by the price. Subsequently, the bond price will be "pulled to par" as the maturity date nears... But another factor is at work. Assuming interest rates remain higher, the future value of reinvested coupon payments goes up... The key detail to note in Exhibit 1 is that at some point in time, the two effects — the price effect and the coupon reinvestment effect — cancel each other out. The remarkable result is that this point in time turns out to be the bond's Macaulay duration (for a zero-coupon bond, its Macaulay duration is its maturity). Therefore, an investor having an investment horizon equal to the bond's Macaulay duration is effectively protected, or immunized, from interest rate risk in that price, and coupon reinvestment effects offset for either higher or lower rates.'

questions.stem: *Money duration* vs *modified duration* *most likely*:

options.A: money duration = modified duration × price.
options.B: same.
options.C: no distinction.

rationales.A:
Full statement: money duration = modified duration × price; drop in bond value from rate shift is estimated by money duration; money duration also known as 'dollar duration' — modified duration multiplied by market value; basis point value (BPV) is money duration multiplied by 1 bp (or 0.0001); closely related risk measure is present value of a basis point (PVBP), also called PV01 in North America (DV01).

Correct. Per the reading: 'That drop in value is estimated by the money duration of the bond. Recall that the money duration is the bond's modified duration statistic multiplied by the price.' Also: 'Money duration, or "dollar duration," is the portfolio modified duration multiplied by the market value... To keep the numbers manageable, we use the basis point value (BPV) measure for money duration. This measure is the money duration multiplied by 1 bp. The BPV is EUR117,824 (= EUR1,178,237,935 × 0.0001)... A closely related risk measure is the present value of a basis point (PVBP), also called the PV01 (present value of an "01," meaning 1 bp) and, in North America, the DV01 (dollar value of an "01").'
rationales.B: Distinct measures.
rationales.C: Real distinction.

questions.stem: The *EUR 250M single liability example* Exhibit 3 portfolio *most likely*:

options.A: no example.
options.B: EUR 250M liability due 15 Feb 2027 (6-year horizon from 15 Feb 2021).
options.C: no example.

rationales.A: Detailed portfolio.
rationales.B:
Full statement: EUR 250M liability due 15 Feb 2027 (6-year horizon from 15 Feb 2021); three-bond portfolio: 2.5-year 1.50% coupon (MacDur 2.463, allocation 23.55%), 7-year 3.25% coupon (MacDur 6.316, allocation 48.52%), 10-year 5.00% coupon (MacDur 7.995, allocation 27.93%); portfolio market value EUR 200,052,250; cash flow yield 3.7608%; Macaulay duration matched to 6-year horizon.

Correct. Per the reading Exhibit 3 (The Bond Portfolio to Immunize the Single Liability): '2.5-Year Bond: Coupon rate 1.50%, Maturity 15 August 2023, Price 100.25, YTM 1.3979%, Par 47,000,000, Market value 47,117,500, MacDur 2.463, Convexity 7.253, Allocation 23.55%. 7-Year Bond: 3.25%, 15 February 2028, 99.75, 3.2903%, 97,300,000, 97,056,750, 6.316, 44.257, 48.52%. 10-Year Bond: 5.00%, 15 February 2031, 100.50, 4.9360%, 55,600,000, 55,878,000, 7.995, 73.747, 27.93%.' Portfolio market value: 'The portfolio's current market value is EUR200,052,250.'
rationales.C: Real data.

questions.stem: The *cash flow yield* used in immunization *most likely*:

options.A: no concept.
options.B: no relation.
options.C: is the internal rate of return on the portfolio's cash flows.

rationales.A: IRR of CFs.
rationales.B: CF yield = HPR.
rationales.C:
Full statement: is the internal rate of return on the portfolio's cash flows; the holding period rate of return (or horizon yield) equals cash flow yield; multi-bond version of well-known result for single bond: realized rate of return matches yield to maturity only if coupon payments are reinvested at that same yield and if bond is held to maturity or sold at a point on constant-yield price trajectory.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The six-year holding period rate of return (ROR), also called the horizon yield, is 3.7608%. It is based on the original market value and the total return and is the solution for ROR: 200,052,250 = 250,167,000 / (1 + ROR/2)^12; ROR = 0.037608. The holding period rate of return equals the cash flow yield for the portfolio. This equivalence is the multi-bond version of the well-known result for a single bond: The realized rate of return matches the yield to maturity only if coupon payments are reinvested at that same yield and if the bond is held to maturity or sold at a point on the constant-yield price trajectory.'

questions.stem: *Immunized portfolio convexity* (Equation 1) *most likely*:

options.A: Immunized portfolio convexity = (MacDur² + MacDur + Dispersion) / (1 + Cash flow yield)².
options.B: no formula.
options.C: no formula.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Immunized portfolio convexity = (MacDur² + MacDur + Dispersion) / (1 + Cash flow yield)²; portfolio dispersion and convexity statistics used to assess structural risk to interest rate immunization strategy; structural risk arises from potential for shifts and twists to the yield curve.

Correct. Per Equation 1: 'Immunized portfolio convexity = (MacDur² + MacDur + Dispersion) / (1 + Cash flow yield)²'. And the reading: 'In terms of semi-annual periods, the Macaulay duration for this portfolio is 12.0008, the dispersion is 33.0378, and the cash flow yield is 1.8804%. Immunized portfolio convexity = (12.0008² + 12.0008 + 33.0378) / (1.018804)² = 182.1437. The portfolio dispersion and convexity statistics are used to assess the structural risk to the interest rate immunization strategy. Structural risk arises from the potential for shifts and twists to the yield curve.'
rationales.B: Explicit formula.
rationales.C: Formula given.

questions.stem: *Parallel yield curve shift* condition for immunization *most likely*:

options.A: A parallel yield curve shift is a necessary condition for immunization to succeed — without it, immunization always fails.
options.B: a sufficient, but not necessary, condition for immunization is a parallel (or shape-preserving) shift whereby all yields change by the same amount in the same direction.
options.C: Yield curve shifts bear no relation to whether an immunized portfolio achieves its target return.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Parallel (shape-preserving) shifts are SUFFICIENT but not necessary — immunization can succeed under some non-parallel movements.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a sufficient, but not necessary, condition for immunization is a parallel (or shape-preserving) shift whereby all yields change by the same amount in the same direction; if change in cash flow yield is the same as that on zero-coupon bond being replicated, immunization can be achieved even with non-parallel shift to yield curve.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'A sufficient, but not necessary, condition for immunization is a parallel (or shape-preserving) shift whereby all yields change by the same amount in the same direction. If the change in the cash flow yield is the same as that on the zero-coupon bond being replicated, immunization can be achieved even with a non-parallel shift to the yield curve.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Curve movements are precisely the risk immunization manages; the sufficiency condition concerns them directly.

questions.stem: *Structural risk* to immunization *most likely* is:

options.A: no risk.
options.B: no concept.
options.C: arises from potential for shifts and twists to yield curve.

rationales.A: Real risk.
rationales.B: Structural risk exists.
rationales.C:
Full statement: arises from potential for shifts and twists to yield curve; minimized by selecting portfolio with lower convexity (and lower dispersion of cash flows); if portfolio's convexity is high, cash flow dispersion is high — greater risk to immunization from non-parallel shifts.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The portfolio dispersion and convexity statistics are used to assess the structural risk to the interest rate immunization strategy. Structural risk arises from the potential for shifts and twists to the yield curve.' Also from a boxed example: 'This risk is minimized by selecting the portfolio with the lower convexity (and dispersion of cash flows).'

questions.stem: *Rebalancing* immunization portfolio *most likely*:

options.A: as time passes and bond yields change, the duration of the bonds changes and portfolio needs to be rebalanced.
options.B: no rebalancing.
options.C: static portfolio.

rationales.A:
Full statement: as time passes and bond yields change, the duration of the bonds changes and portfolio needs to be rebalanced; can be accomplished by buying and selling bonds or using interest rate derivatives (futures contracts and interest rate swaps).

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'In the case of a single liability, immunization is achieved by matching the Macaulay duration of the bond portfolio to the horizon date. As time passes and bond yields change, the duration of the bonds changes and the portfolio needs to be rebalanced. This rebalancing can be accomplished by buying and selling bonds or using interest rate derivatives, such as futures contracts and interest rate swaps.'
rationales.B: Ongoing rebalancing.
rationales.C: Rebalancing needed.

questions.stem: *Immunization aims to lock in* *most likely*:

options.A: no target.
options.B: cash flow yield on the portfolio, which is the internal rate of return on the cash flows.
options.C: no goal.

rationales.A: Cash flow yield.
rationales.B:
Full statement: cash flow yield on the portfolio, which is the internal rate of return on the cash flows; it is NOT the weighted average of the yields to maturity on the bonds that constitute the portfolio.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'An immunization strategy aims to lock in the cash flow yield on the portfolio, which is the internal rate of return on the cash flows. It is not the weighted average of the yields to maturity on the bonds that constitute the portfolio.'
rationales.C: IRR lock-in.

drills.los_text: compare strategies for a single liability and for multiple liabilities, including alternative means of implementation

questions.stem: *Multiple liability immunization vs single liability* *most likely*:

options.A: same conditions.
options.B: no distinction.
options.C: principle applies to multiple liabilities in addition to single liability.

rationales.A: Slight difference.
rationales.B: Distinct condition.
rationales.C:
Full statement: principle applies to multiple liabilities in addition to single liability; unlike single liability case where asset portfolio's modified duration (money duration) must be slightly greater than that of liability, immunization against multiple liabilities requires that asset portfolio's money duration (BPV) match that of liabilities — match may be slightly above or below provided it is closest available to liability BPV.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The principle of interest rate immunization applies to multiple liabilities in addition to a single liability... Recall that in the case of a single liability, the immunization strategy is to match the portfolio Macaulay duration with the investment horizon. Also, the initial investment needs to match (or exceed) the present value of the liability. These two conditions can be combined to prescribe that the money duration of the immunizing portfolio matches the money duration of the debt liabilities... With multiple liabilities, matching money durations is useful because the market values and cash flow yields of the assets and liabilities are not necessarily equal. Unlike the single liability case, where the asset portfolio's modified duration (money duration) must be slightly greater than that of the liability, immunization against multiple liabilities requires that the asset portfolio's money duration (BPV) match that of the liabilities. The match may be slightly above or below, provided it is the closest available to the liability BPV.'

questions.stem: *Conditions to immunize multiple liabilities* *most likely* require:

options.A: (1) market value of assets is greater than or equal to market value of liabilities.
options.B: single condition.
options.C: no conditions.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) market value of assets is greater than or equal to market value of liabilities; (2) asset basis point value (BPV) equals liability BPV; (3) dispersion of cash flows and convexity of assets are greater than those of the liabilities.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'The conditions to immunize multiple liabilities are that (1) the market value of assets is greater than or equal to the market value of the liabilities, (2) the asset basis point value (BPV) equals the liability BPV, and (3) the dispersion of cash flows and the convexity of assets are greater than those of the liabilities.'
rationales.B: Three conditions.
rationales.C: Three requirements.

questions.stem: *Duration matching approach* three alternatives to manage liabilities *most likely* include:

options.A: no options.
options.B: (1) duration matching — extends ideas from single liability to portfolio of debt liabilities.
options.C: single option.

rationales.A: Three approaches.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) duration matching — extends ideas from single liability to portfolio of debt liabilities; (2) derivatives overlay — using futures contracts on government bonds in immunization strategy; (3) contingent immunization — allows for active bond portfolio management as long as surplus is above designated threshold.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In this section, we discuss several approaches to manage these liabilities: Duration matching, which extends the ideas of the previous section to a portfolio of debt liabilities. Derivatives overlay, in particular using futures contracts on government bonds in the immunization strategy; and Contingent immunization, which allows for active bond portfolio management as long as the surplus is above a designated threshold.'
rationales.C: Three methods.

questions.stem: *Number of futures contracts for immunization* Equation 3 *most likely*:

options.A: no formula.
options.B: no formula.
options.C: N_f = (Liability portfolio BPV − Asset portfolio BPV) / Futures BPV.

rationales.A: Explicit formula.
rationales.B: Formula given.
rationales.C:
Full statement: N_f = (Liability portfolio BPV − Asset portfolio BPV) / Futures BPV; if N_f positive, asset manager buys (goes long) required number; if N_f negative, asset manager sells (goes short) to reduce money duration; futures BPV approximated as BPV_CTD / CF_CTD where CTD is cheapest-to-deliver security.

Correct. Per Equations 3 and 4: 'N_f = (Liability portfolio BPV − Asset portfolio BPV) / Futures BPV. If Nf is a positive number, the asset manager buys, or goes long, the required number of futures contracts. Doing so raises the money duration of the assets to match that of the liabilities. If Nf is a negative number, the asset manager sells, or goes short, futures contracts to reduce the money duration.' And: 'To simplify, we use an approximation formula that is common in practice: Futures BPV ≈ BPV_CTD / CF_CTD where CF_CTD is the conversion factor for the CTD security.'

questions.stem: The *US Treasury futures example* — asset BPV 18,819, liability BPV 117,824 *most likely*:

options.B: The Treasury futures example concludes that no futures position can close the BPV gap between assets and liabilities.
options.C: The example poses the hedge but leaves the required number of contracts uncomputed.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if CTD is 6.5-year T-note (BPV 56.8727, CF 0.8226), futures BPV = 56.8727/0.8226 = 69.1377 and required contracts = (117,824 - 18,819)/69.1377 = 1,432; but if CTD is 10-year T-note (BPV 81.6607, CF 0.8516), futures BPV = 95.8909 and required contracts = 1,032; difference of 400 contracts significant — must know CTD.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If the CTD security is the 6.5-year T-note shown in Exhibit 17, the futures BPV is estimated to be USD69.1377 (= 56.8727/0.8226). Then, the required number of contracts is approximately 1,432: (117,824 − 18,819)/69.1377 = 1,432. But if the CTD security is the 10-year T-note, the futures BPV is USD95.8909 (= 81.6607/0.8516). To close the money duration gap, the required number of contracts is only 1,032: (117,824 − 18,819)/95.8909 = 1,032. Clearly, the asset manager must know the CTD T-note to use in the derivatives overlay strategy. The difference of 400 futures contracts is significant.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The gap is hedgeable — the computation delivers the required contract count using the CTD's BPV and conversion factor.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The calculation is completed: futures BPV = 56.8727/0.8226 = 69.1377, contracts = (117,824 − 18,819)/69.1377.

questions.stem: *Conversion factors* for T-note futures *most likely*:

options.A: always 1.0000.
options.B: based on arbitrary yield to maturity of 6.00%.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: 6% assumption.
rationales.B:
Full statement: based on arbitrary yield to maturity of 6.00%; if eligible T-note has coupon rate below (above) 6.00%, conversion factor is less (more) than 1.0000; invoice price paid by buyer at expiration is futures price × conversion factor + accrued interest; when yields are below 6.00%, CTD security typically is qualifying T-note having lowest duration (10-year T-note futures effectively acts as 6.5-year contract, motivating introduction of Ultra 10-year contract).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Conversion factors that are used to make the qualifying T-notes roughly equivalent for delivery by the contract seller, or short position are based on an arbitrary yield to maturity of 6.00%. If the eligible T-note has a coupon rate below (above) 6.00%, the conversion factor is less (more) than 1.0000. The invoice price paid by the buyer of the contract, the long position, at the expiration of the contract is the futures price multiplied by the conversion factor, plus accrued interest.' Also: 'A factor in determining the CTD T-note is that the conversion factors for each qualifying security are based on the arbitrary assumption of a 6.00% yield to maturity. In practice, when yields are below 6.00% the CTD security typically is the qualifying T-note having the lowest duration. Therefore, the 10-year T-note futures contract essentially has been acting as a 6.5-year contract. (That explains the motivation for introducing the Ultra 10-year contract — to provide a hedging instrument more closely tied to the 10-year T-note traded in the cash market.)'
rationales.C: Real mechanism.

questions.stem: *Contingent immunization* *most likely*:

options.A: no such strategy.
options.B: no strategy.
options.C: hybrid passive-active strategy.

rationales.A: Hybrid strategy.
rationales.B: Real strategy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: hybrid passive-active strategy; asset manager can pursue active investment strategies as if operating under total return mandate if surplus is above designated threshold; if actively managed assets perform poorly and surplus evaporates, mandate reverts to purely passive duration-matching portfolio; when surplus above threshold, manager may increase portfolio risk in any asset category (equity, fixed income, alternatives, out-of-money commodity options, credit default swaps); liquidity important criterion because positions unwound if losses near threshold.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The presence of a significant surplus allows the asset manager to consider a hybrid passive–active strategy known as contingent immunization. The idea behind contingent immunization is that the asset manager can pursue active investment strategies, as if operating under a total return mandate, if the surplus is above a designated threshold. If the actively managed assets perform poorly, however, and the surplus evaporates, the mandate reverts to the purely passive strategy of building a duration-matching portfolio and then managing it to remain on duration target. In principle, when the surplus is above a sufficient threshold, the manager may increase portfolio risk in any asset category, including equity, fixed income, and alternative investments. The manager could also buy out-of-the-money commodity options contracts or credit default swaps. The objective is to attain portfolio gains to reduce the cost of retiring the debt obligations without falling below the minimum funding threshold. Obviously, liquidity is an important criterion in selecting the investments because the positions will need to be unwound if losses cause the surplus to near the threshold.'

questions.stem: *Case study — Japanese corporation* three alternatives *most likely*:

options.A: (1) bond tender offer — buy back debt liabilities on open market at premium above market price, at price commensurate with triple-A rating to entice bondholders.
options.B: The Japanese corporation case presents a single course of action: holding the existing debt to maturity.
options.C: The case identifies no alternatives for managing the corporation's debt liabilities.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) bond tender offer — buy back debt liabilities on open market at premium above market price, at price commensurate with triple-A rating to entice bondholders; (2) cash flow matching — buys portfolio of government bonds matching coupon interest and principal on debt liabilities, external auditors would agree to accounting defeasement allowing removal from balance sheet; (3) duration matching — buys portfolio of high-quality corporate bonds matching duration, using interest rate derivatives to keep duration on target; auditors unlikely to allow accounting defeasement.

Correct. Per the case study: 'An investment bank working with the corporation offers three alternatives to accomplish the objective: 1. Bond tender offer. The corporation would buy back the debt liabilities on the open market, paying a premium above the market price... The investment bank anticipates that the tender offer would have to be at a price commensurate with a triple-A rating to entice the bondholders to sell. 2. Cash flow matching. The corporation buys a portfolio of government bonds that matches, as closely as possible, the coupon interest and principal redemptions on the debt liabilities. The investment bank is highly confident that the corporation's external auditors will agree to accounting defeasement because the purchased bonds are government securities. That agreement will allow the corporation to remove both the defeasing asset portfolio and the liabilities from the balance sheet. 3. Duration matching. The corporation buys a portfolio of high-quality corporate bonds that matches the duration of the debt liabilities. Interest rate derivatives contracts will be used to keep the duration on its target as time passes and yields change. The investment bank thinks it is very unlikely that the external auditors will allow this strategy to qualify for accounting defeasement.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Three alternatives are developed — the tender offer is one of them, alongside cash-flow matching and duration matching.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The case is built around comparing three distinct defeasance approaches.

questions.stem: *Portfolio selection for duration matching* *most likely*:

options.A: highest BPV.
options.B: duration matching when market values of assets and liabilities differ entails matching money durations (BPVs).
options.C: no criteria.

rationales.A: Complex criteria.
rationales.B:
Full statement: duration matching when market values of assets and liabilities differ entails matching money durations (BPVs); important difference between portfolios lies in convexities — to immunize multiple liabilities, convexity (and dispersion of cash flows) of assets needs to be greater than liabilities BUT must have lowest possible convexity available to minimize structural risk to strategy; correct immunizing portfolio is one with lower convexity that still exceeds liabilities.

Correct. Per the case study solution: 'Duration matching when the market values of the assets and liabilities differ, however, entails matching the money durations, in particular the BPVs. The choice then comes down to Portfolios A and B. Although both have BPVs close to the liabilities, it is incorrect to choose A based on its BPV being "closer." The important difference between Portfolios A and B lies in the convexities. To immunize multiple liabilities, the convexity (and dispersion of cash flows) of the assets needs to be greater than the liabilities. Therefore, Portfolio A does not meet that condition. Recall that in an earlier exercise, the correct immunizing portfolio is the one with the lower convexity, which minimizes the structural risk to the strategy. But, that bond portfolio still has a convexity greater than the zero-coupon bond that would provide perfect immunization.' Also: 'in addition to matching money duration, immunization against multiple liabilities requires an asset portfolio with convexity which exceeds that of the liabilities but must have the lowest possible convexity available in order to minimize the structural risk to the strategy.'
rationales.C: Convexity balance.

questions.stem: *Synthetic barbell strategy* *most likely*:

options.A: no such strategy.
options.B: no concept.
options.C: having positions in short-term and longer-term segments of yield curve.

rationales.A: Synthetic barbell.
rationales.B: Real strategy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: having positions in short-term and longer-term segments of yield curve; 'synthetic' means 'created with derivatives'; underlying asset portfolio concentrated in short-term market; derivatives portfolio at 6.5-year or 10-year segment; CME Group also has 2-year and 5-year Treasury futures contracts, so asset manager could spread futures across other segments — that diversification reduces structural risk to immunization strategy from non-parallel shifts and twists.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The asset manager has established a synthetic "barbell" strategy: having positions in the short-term and longer-term segments of the yield curve. The term "synthetic" means "created with derivatives." The underlying asset portfolio is concentrated in the short-term market. The derivatives portfolio is either at the 6.5-year or 10-year segment of the yield curve. CME Group also has actively traded two-year and five-year Treasury futures contracts. Therefore, the asset manager could choose to spread out the futures contracts across other segments of the yield curve. That diversification reduces the structural risk to the immunization strategy arising from non-parallel shifts and twists to the curve.'

questions.stem: *Type I cash flows* *most likely* are:

options.A: cash flows where scheduled amounts and payment dates are known to asset manager.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no distinction.

rationales.A:
Full statement: cash flows where scheduled amounts and payment dates are known to asset manager; assumed in single liability and multiple liability immunization contexts; more, and harder-to-make, assumptions needed to deal with Type IV liabilities and lead to greater uncertainty regarding models' outputs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For now, we continue to assume that these are Type I cash flows in that the scheduled amounts and payment dates are known to the asset manager.' Also: 'Measurement error for asset BPV can even arise in the classic immunization strategy for Type I cash flows, which have set amounts and dates.' And: 'More, and harder-to-make, assumptions are needed to deal with Type IV liabilities and lead to greater uncertainty regarding the models' outputs.'
rationales.B: Type I definition.
rationales.C: Known dates + amounts.

drills.los_text: evaluate liability-based strategies under various interest rate scenarios and select a strategy to achieve a portfolio's objectives

questions.stem: The *pension plan asset BPV calculation* — 50% equity, 40% fixed income (MD 5.5), 10% alternatives on $2.7B *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.C: no calc.

rationales.A: Weighted calc.
rationales.B:
Asset BPV = $2.700B × [(0.50 × 0) + (0.40 × 5.5) + (0.10 × 0)] × 0.0001 = $594,000 (term in brackets is estimated effective duration for asset portfolio using shares of market value as weights); equity and alternatives assumed to have zero effective duration because there is no stable and predictable relationship between valuations on those asset classes and market interest rates; assuming zero duration does not imply no interest rate risk.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Given these assumptions, we conclude that the asset BPV is USD594,000 = USD2.700 billion × [(0.50 × 0) + (0.40 × 5.5) + (0.10 × 0)] × 0.0001. The term in brackets is the estimated effective duration for the asset portfolio, calculated using the shares of market value as the weights. Clearly, the pension plan is running a significant duration gap — the asset BPV of USD594,000 is much lower than the liability BPV of USD2,257,080, using the PBO measure.' Also: 'To be conservative, we assume that there is no stable and predictable relationship between valuations on those asset classes and market interest rates. Therefore, equity duration and alternatives duration are assumed to be zero. Assuming zero duration does not imply that equity and alternatives have no interest rate risk.'
rationales.C: Weighted duration.

questions.curriculum_ref: Interest Rate Scenarios — asset BPV calc

questions.stem: *Duration gap using futures* — Ultra 10-year T-note contract, liability BPV 2,257,080, asset BPV 594,000, futures BPV 95.8909 *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.B: no calc.
options.C: $8.315 million.

rationales.A: Futures calc.
rationales.B: 17,343 contracts.
rationales.C:
N_f = (2,257,080 - 594,000) / 95.8909 = 17,343 contracts to fully hedge; one concern with futures hedging is need for daily oversight because futures marked to market and settled at end of each day into margin account — 5 bps rate move creates realized loss of $95.8909 × 5 × 17,343 = $8.315 million offset by unrealized reduction in PV of liabilities; large futures position leads to significant daily cash flows.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, suppose the Ultra 10-year Treasury futures contract at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange has a BPV of USD95.8909 because the on-the-run T-note is the CTD security. Using Equation 3, the pension plan would need to buy, or go long, 17,343 contracts to fully hedge the interest rate risk created by the duration gap: N_f = (2,257,080 − 594,000)/95.8909 = 17,343. One concern with hedging with futures is the need for daily oversight of the positions. That need arises because futures contracts are marked to market and settled at the end of each trading day into the margin account. Suppose that the fund did buy 17,343 futures contracts and 10-year Treasury yields go up by 5 bps. Given that the futures BPV is USD95.8909 per contract, the realized loss that day is more than USD8.315 million: USD95.8909 × 5 × 17,343 = 8,315,179.'

questions.curriculum_ref: Interest Rate Scenarios — futures hedge calc

questions.stem: *Interest rate swap for duration gap* Equation 5 *most likely*:

options.A: Asset BPV + [NP × Swap BPV/100] = Liability BPV.
options.B: no formula.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Asset BPV + [NP × Swap BPV/100] = Liability BPV; from pension fund perspective, receive-fixed swap = buying 30-year fixed-rate bond from swap dealer and financing purchase by issuing 30-year FRN paying 3-month MRR; swap's money duration = (high) duration of fixed-rate bond minus (low) duration of FRN — receive-fixed swap has positive duration; from swap dealer's perspective, contract is purchasing (low duration) FRN financed by issuing (high duration) fixed-rate bond so swap has negative duration to dealer.

Correct. Per Equation 5: 'Asset BPV + [NP × Swap BPV/100] = Liability BPV.' And the reading: 'The risk statistics for an interest rate swap can be obtained from interpreting the contract as a combination of bonds. From the pension fund's perspective, the swap is viewed as buying a 30-year, 4.16% fixed-rate bond from the swap dealer and financing that purchase by issuing a 30-year floating-rate note (FRN) that pays the three-month MRR.' And: 'The swap's money duration is taken to be the (high) duration of the fixed-rate bond minus the (low) duration of the FRN. That explains why a receive-fixed swap has positive duration. From the swap dealer's perspective, the contract is viewed as purchasing a (low duration) FRN that is financed by issuing a (high duration) fixed-rate bond. Hence, the swap has negative duration to the dealer.'
rationales.B: Explicit formula.
rationales.C: Swap = bonds combo.

questions.stem: *Notional principal calculation* — 30-year receive-fixed swap 4.16% BPV 0.1673 *most likely*:

options.A: no answer.
options.C: no calc.

rationales.A: $994M notional.
rationales.B:
Full statement: 594,000 + [NP × 0.1673/100] = 2,257,080; NP = 994,070,532 (approximately $994 million); receive-fixed swap has positive duration; if swap rates fall, receive-fixed gains value; hedging fund uses this to offset losses on balance sheet from falling rates.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The notional principal (NP) on the interest rate swap needed to close the duration gap to zero can be calculated with this expression: Asset BPV + [NP × Swap BPV/100] = Liability BPV. This is similar to Equation 2 for futures contracts. Given that the Asset BPV is USD594,000 and the Liability BPV is USD2,257,080 using the PBO measure, the required notional principal for the receive-fixed swap having a BPV of 0.1673 is about USD994 million. 594,000 + [NP × 0.1673/100] = 2,257,080; NP = 994,070,532.'
rationales.C: Concrete NP.

questions.stem: *Hedging ratio* concept *most likely*:

options.A: no concept.
options.B: no concept.
options.C: extent of interest rate risk management.

rationales.A: Real concept.
rationales.C:
Full statement: extent of interest rate risk management; hedging ratio of 0% indicates no hedging at all (plan retains significant negative duration gap); hedging ratio of 100% indicates full immunization; in practice partial hedges are common; manager's task is to select ratio between 0% and 100%; interest rate swaps typically have value of zero at initiation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'We use the term "hedging ratio" (or "interest rate hedging ratio," since the focus is on reducing interest rate risk) to indicate the extent of interest rate risk management. A hedging ratio of 0% indicates no hedging at all. The pension plan retains the significant negative duration gap and the risk of lower corporate bond yields if it does not hedge. A hedging ratio of 100% indicates an attempt to fully balance, or to immunize, the assets and liabilities. In this case, the plan manager enters the receive-fixed swap for a notional principal of USD994 million. In practice, partial hedges are common; the manager's task is to select the hedging ratio between 0% and 100%.'

questions.stem: *Strategic hedging* *most likely*:

options.A: active management of the hedging ratio.
options.B: no concept.
options.C: no concept.

rationales.A:
Full statement: active management of the hedging ratio; plan sponsor allows manager flexibility in selecting hedging ratio (e.g., mandate to stay within range of 25% to 75%); when manager anticipates lower market rates and gains on receive-fixed swaps, prefers to be at top of allowable range; if market swap rates expected to go up, could reduce hedging ratio to lower end; performance measured against strategy of maintaining preset hedging ratio (e.g., 50%).

Correct. Per the reading: 'One possibility is that the plan sponsor allows the manager some flexibility (called "strategic hedging") in selecting the hedging ratio. For example, the mandate could be to stay within a range of 25% to 75%. When the manager anticipates lower market rates and gains on receive-fixed interest rate swaps, the manager prefers to be at the top of an allowable range. On the other hand, if market (swap) rates are expected to go up, the manager could reduce the hedging ratio to the lower end of the range. The performance of the strategic hedging decisions can be measured against a strategy of maintaining a preset hedging ratio, for instance, 50%.'
rationales.B: Active management.
rationales.C: Range flexibility.

questions.stem: *Alternatives to receive-fixed swap* for pension fund duration gap *most likely* include:

options.A: No alternatives to the receive-fixed swap exist for closing a pension duration gap.
options.B: (1) purchased receiver swaption — confers right to buyer to enter swap as fixed-rate receiver, gain on receiver swaption as rates decline offsets losses on balance sheet, but requires paying option premium up front.
options.C: The only alternative to the swap is selling the pension's equity holdings to buy long bonds.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A purchased receiver swaption and a swaption collar are both developed as alternatives.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) purchased receiver swaption — confers right to buyer to enter swap as fixed-rate receiver, gain on receiver swaption as rates decline offsets losses on balance sheet, but requires paying option premium up front; (2) swaption collar — combination of buying receiver swaption and writing payer swaption, premium received on payer swaption offsets premium needed to buy receiver swaption.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'An alternative to the receive-fixed interest rate swap is a purchased receiver swaption. This swaption confers to the buyer the right to enter the swap as the fixed-rate receiver. Because of its negative duration gap (asset BPV is less than liability BPV), the typical pension plan suffers when interest rates fall and could become underfunded. The gain on the receiver swaption as rates decline offsets the losses on the balance sheet. Another alternative is a swaption collar, the combination of buying the receiver swaption and writing a payer swaption. The premium received on the payer swaption that is written offsets the premium needed to buy the receiver swaption.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The derivative alternatives — receiver swaption and collar — add hedging BPV without reallocating the asset portfolio.

questions.stem: *Choosing among receive-fixed swap, receiver swaption, and swaption collar* *most likely*:

options.A: no criteria.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: depends in part on pension fund manager's view on future interest rates.

rationales.A: Rate-view driven.
rationales.B: View-based choice.
rationales.C:
Full statement: depends in part on pension fund manager's view on future interest rates; if rates expected to be low, receive-fixed swap typically preferred derivative; if rates expected to go up, receiver swaption can become attractive; if rates projected to reach certain threshold that depends on option costs and strike rates, swaption collar can become favored choice.

Correct. Per the reading Summary: 'The choice among hedging with the receive-fixed swap, the purchased receiver swaption, and the swaption collar depends in part on the pension fund manager's view on future interest rates. If rates are expected to be low, the receive-fixed swap typically is the preferred derivative. If rates are expected to go up, the receiver swaption can become attractive. And if rates are projected to reach a certain threshold that depends on the option costs and the strike rates, the swaption collar can become the favored choice.'

questions.curriculum_ref: Interest Rate Scenarios — hedge instrument choice

questions.stem: *Steepening twist scenario* Exhibit 15 *most likely*:

options.A: short-term yields on high-quality bonds go down while long-term yields go up (1.5-year drops 25 bps, 6-year unchanged, 11.5-year up 25 bps).
options.B: In the steepening twist scenario, hedged and unhedged positions produce identical outcomes across the curve.
options.C: Exhibit 15 presents no scenario data for a steepening twist.

rationales.A:
Full statement: short-term yields on high-quality bonds go down while long-term yields go up (1.5-year drops 25 bps, 6-year unchanged, 11.5-year up 25 bps); leads to loss of EUR1,178,071 in asset portfolio as cash flow yield increases by 10.04 bps; market value of liabilities drops by only EUR835,156 because cash flow yield increases by only 7.11 bps; steepening twist to shape of yield curve is source of loss (difference EUR342,915).

Correct. Per Exhibit 15 and reading: 'This scenario is the steepening twist in which short-term yields on high-quality bonds go down while long-term yields go up. The 1.5-year yield is assumed to drop by 25 bps. The 6-year yield remains the same, and the 11.5-year yield goes up by 25 bps. These changes lead to a loss of EUR1,178,071 in the asset portfolio as the cash flow yield increases by 10.04 bps... The market value of the liabilities drops by only EUR835,156 because the cash flow yield increases by only 7.11 bps. The value of the assets goes down by more than the liabilities — the difference is EUR342,915. The steepening twist to the shape of the yield curve is the source of the loss.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The twist creates differential outcomes precisely because yield changes vary by maturity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The scenario is specified: 1.5-year down 25 bps, 6-year unchanged, 11.5-year up 25 bps.

questions.stem: *ABO vs PBO measures* for pension plan *most likely*:

options.A: same.
options.C: no difference.

rationales.A: Distinct measures.
rationales.B:
accumulated benefit obligation (ABO) based on current wages; projected benefit obligation (PBO) based on expected future wages; example: ABO $1.730B with effective duration 17.1; PBO $2.687B with effective duration 8.4; ABO BPV = $1.730B × 17.1 × 0.0001 = $2,958,300; PBO BPV = $2.687B × 8.4 × 0.0001 = $2,257,080; if corporation anticipates acquisition target with acquirer converting from defined benefit to defined contribution, ABO measure matters more than PBO; for corporate sponsor preserving pension plan design as ongoing independent institution, PBO is appropriate measure.

Correct. Per the reading: 'There is a significant difference between having liabilities of USD1.730 billion and an effective duration of 17.1, as measured by the ABO, and liabilities of USD2.687 billion and an effective duration of 8.4, as measured by the PBO. The ABO BPV is USD2,958,300 (= USD1.730 billion × 17.1 × 0.0001), and the PBO BPV is USD2,257,080 (= USD2.687 billion × 8.4 × 0.0001). The plan sponsor must decide which liability measure to use for risk management and asset allocation. For example, if the corporation anticipates that it might be a target for an acquisition and that the acquirer likely would want to convert the retirement plan from defined benefit to defined contribution, the ABO measure matters more than the PBO. We assume that the corporate sponsor sees itself as an ongoing independent institution that preserves the pension plan's current design. Therefore, PBO is the appropriate measure for pension plan liabilities.'
rationales.C: Distinct measures.

questions.curriculum_ref: Interest Rate Scenarios — ABO vs PBO

drills.los_text: explain risks associated with managing a portfolio against a liability structure

questions.stem: *Essential relationship for full interest rate hedging* Equation 6 *most likely*:

options.A: No formula relates asset, hedge, and liability sensitivities in interest rate hedging.
options.B: Full hedging requires only that asset BPV equal liability BPV, with no role for the hedge position.
options.C: Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields (all yield changes in basis points).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Equation 6 states the relationship explicitly across all three components.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The hedge term is integral: Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields (all yield changes in basis points); describes immunization strategy (hedging ratio of 100%) matching changes in market value on each side of balance sheet; 'approximately equals' sign results from ignoring higher-order terms such as convexity.

Correct. Per Equation 6: 'Asset BPV × ΔAsset yields + Hedge BPV × ΔHedge yields ≈ Liability BPV × ΔLiability yields. ΔAsset yields, ΔHedge yields, and ΔLiability yields are measured in basis points. This equation describes an immunization strategy (a hedging ratio of 100%) whereby the intent is to match the changes in market value on each side of the balance sheet when yields change. Doing so entails matching the money duration of assets and liabilities. We know, however, that entities also choose to partially hedge interest rate risk by selecting a hedging ratio less than 100%. In any case, Equation 6 serves to indicate the source of the risks to LDI. The "approximately equals" sign (≈) in the equation results from ignoring higher-order terms, such as convexity.'

questions.stem: *Model risk* in LDI *most likely*:

options.A: encountered whenever assumptions made about future events and approximations used to measure key parameters.
options.B: no risk.
options.C: no risk.

rationales.A:
Full statement: encountered whenever assumptions made about future events and approximations used to measure key parameters; risk is that assumptions turn out to be wrong and approximations are inaccurate; e.g., assuming effective durations for investments in equity and alternative assets are zero — modeling problem is effect on those asset classes not predictable or stable because depends on reason for change in nominal interest rates.

Correct. Per the reading: 'We encounter model risk in financial modeling whenever assumptions are made about future events and approximations are used to measure key parameters. The risk is that those assumptions turn out to be wrong and the approximations are inaccurate. For example, in our earlier defined benefit pension plan example, we assumed that the effective durations for investments in equity and alternative assets are zero. That assumption introduces the risk that asset BPV is mis-measured if, in fact, those market values change as the yield curve shifts. The modeling problem is that the effect on those asset classes is not predictable or stable because it depends on the reason for the change in nominal interest rates. Unlike fixed-income bonds, an increase in expected inflation can have a very different effect on equity and alternative asset valuations than an increase in the real rate.'
rationales.B: Real risk.
rationales.C: Assumption risk.

questions.stem: *Asset BPV measurement error* even for Type I cash flows *most likely*:

options.A: Asset BPV can be measured without error whenever the liability cash flows are Type I.
options.B: in practice common to approximate asset portfolio duration using weighted average of individual durations for component bonds.
options.C: Duration approximation introduces no measurement issue in immunization practice.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Even with certain (Type I) cash flows, the common weighted-average-duration approximation introduces error.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in practice common to approximate asset portfolio duration using weighted average of individual durations for component bonds; a better approach to achieve immunization uses cash flow yield to discount future coupon and principal payments; this error is minimized when underlying yield curve is flat or when future cash flows are concentrated in flattest segment of curve.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Measurement error for asset BPV can even arise in the classic immunization strategy for Type I cash flows, which have set amounts and dates. In practice, it is common to approximate the asset portfolio duration using the weighted average of the individual durations for the component bonds. A better approach to achieve immunization, however, uses the cash flow yield to discount the future coupon and principal payments. This error is minimized when the underlying yield curve is flat or when future cash flows are concentrated in the flattest segment of the curve.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The weighted-average shortcut ignores curve shape effects — a real, acknowledged measurement error.

questions.stem: *Hedge BPV measurement error* *most likely*:

options.A: Hedge BPV is known exactly because futures contracts have contractually fixed sensitivities.
options.B: Derivatives overlays introduce no measurement risk into the hedging relationship.
options.C: when using derivatives overlays to immunize, Equation 4 estimates futures BPV to be BPV for CTD security divided by its conversion factor.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Futures BPV is ESTIMATED — CTD BPV divided by its conversion factor — and the CTD itself can change.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The estimation chain (CTD identification, conversion factor) is a genuine source of hedge measurement error.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when using derivatives overlays to immunize, Equation 4 estimates futures BPV to be BPV for CTD security divided by its conversion factor; a more developed calculation involving short-term rates and accrued interest could change number of contracts needed to hedge interest rate risk; although error introduced by using approximation might not be large, still can be source of underperformance in hedging strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A similar problem arises in measuring hedge BPV. When we illustrated the use of derivatives overlays to immunize, we used a common approximation for the futures BPV. Equation 4 estimates it to be the BPV for the qualifying CTD security divided by its conversion factor. A more developed calculation involving short-term rates and accrued interest, however, could change the number of contracts needed to hedge the interest rate risk. Although the error introduced by using an approximation might not be large, it still can be a source of underperformance in the hedging strategy.'

questions.stem: *Liability BPV model risk in pension plan example* *most likely*:

options.A: measuring pension plan's liability is difficult financial modeling problem.
options.B: no assumptions.
options.C: no risk.

rationales.A:
Full statement: measuring pension plan's liability is difficult financial modeling problem; simple models for two liability measures (ABO and PBO) necessarily require many assumptions about future including dates when employees retire and their wage levels at those times; difficulty in projecting life spans of retirees leads to longevity risk (sponsor has not provided sufficient assets to make longer-than-expected payout stream); more, and harder-to-make, assumptions needed to deal with Type IV liabilities and lead to greater uncertainty.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Model risk in obtaining a measure of liability BPV is evident in the earlier defined benefit pension plan example. Measuring a defined benefit pension plan's liability is clearly a difficult financial modeling problem. Even the simple models for the two liability measures (the ABO and PBO) necessarily require many assumptions about the future, including the dates when employees retire and their wage levels at those times. The difficulty in projecting life spans of retirees covered by the pension plan leads to longevity risk. The risk is the sponsor has not provided sufficient assets to make the longer-than-expected payout stream. More, and harder-to-make, assumptions are needed to deal with Type IV liabilities and lead to greater uncertainty regarding the models' outputs.'
rationales.B: Multi-assumption.
rationales.C: Real risks.

questions.stem: *Non-parallel shifts risk* *most likely*:

options.A: no risk.
options.B: implicit in Equation 6 is assumption that all yields change by same number of basis points.
options.C: no risk.

rationales.A: Non-parallel risk.
rationales.B:
Full statement: implicit in Equation 6 is assumption that all yields change by same number of basis points; strong assumption and source of risk if fixed-income assets, derivatives, and liabilities are positioned at varying points along benchmark yield curve; parallel yield curve shift is sufficient but not necessary condition; non-parallel shifts and twists can result in changes to cash flow yield that don't match change in zero-coupon bond yield; minimizing dispersion of cash flows in asset portfolio mitigates this risk.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Implicit in Equation 6 is the assumption that all yields change by the same number of basis points — that is, ΔAsset Yields, ΔHedge Yields, and ΔLiability Yields are equal. That is a strong assumption — and a source of risk — if the fixed-income assets, the derivatives, and the liabilities are positioned at varying points along the benchmark bond yield curve and at varying spreads to that curve. Previously, when we discussed immunizing the interest rate risk on a single liability by structuring and managing a portfolio of fixed-income bonds, we pointed out that a parallel yield curve shift is a sufficient but not necessary condition to achieve the desired outcome. Non-parallel shifts and twists to the yield curve can result in changes to the cash flow yield on the immunizing portfolio that do not match the change in the yield on the zero-coupon bond that provides perfect immunization. Minimizing dispersion of the cash flows in the asset portfolio mitigates this risk.'
rationales.C: Real risk.

questions.stem: *Spread risk in LDI* *most likely*:

options.A: Spread risk does not arise in LDI because assets and liabilities respond to the same yield curve.
options.B: LDI frameworks are immune to divergence between asset and liability discount rates.
options.C: framework rests on changes in benchmark bond yield curve (usually government bonds), but in practice ΔAsset yield and ΔLiability yield often refer to various classes of corporate bonds.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Asset yields and liability discount rates often move by DIFFERENT amounts than benchmark government yields — that divergence is spread risk.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The framework rests on the benchmark curve, but corporate-quality liability discounting and asset spreads can decouple from it.
rationales.C:
Full statement: framework rests on changes in benchmark bond yield curve (usually government bonds), but in practice ΔAsset yield and ΔLiability yield often refer to various classes of corporate bonds; risk is that respective spreads on broad index and high-quality sector do not move in unison with shift in government bond yield curve; similar spread risk present in immunizing multiple Type I liabilities.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Generally, the framework for thinking about interest rate risk rests on changes in the benchmark bond yield curve, which usually is the yield curve for government bonds. In practice, however, ΔAsset yield and ΔLiability yield often refer to various classes of corporate bonds. In the pension fund example, the fund holds a portfolio of fixed-income bonds that tracks a well-diversified index of corporate bonds that may include non-investment-grade securities. The present value of retirement benefits, however, depends on yields on high-quality corporate bonds. Therefore, a risk is that the respective spreads on the broad index and the high-quality sector do not move in unison with a shift in the government bond yield curve. A similar spread risk is present in the earlier example of immunizing multiple Type I liabilities.'

questions.stem: *Treasury vs corporate volatility for hedging* *most likely*:

options.A: usually yields on high-quality corporate bonds are less volatile than on more-liquid Treasuries.
options.B: identical.
options.C: no distinction.

rationales.A:
Full statement: usually yields on high-quality corporate bonds are less volatile than on more-liquid Treasuries; government bonds used in wide variety of hedging as well as speculative trading strategies by institutional investors; also, inflows of international funds typically placed in government bonds at least until allocated to other asset classes; those factors lead to greater volatility in Treasury yields than comparable-maturity corporate bonds.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Spread risk also is apparent in the derivatives overlay LDI strategies. We illustrated how futures contracts can be used to hedge the interest rate risk of the multiple liabilities, either passively or contingently. In particular, the futures contracts are on 10-year US Treasury notes, whereas the liabilities are corporate obligations. Movements in the corporate/Treasury yield spread introduce risk to the hedging strategy. Usually, yields on high-quality corporate bonds are less volatile than on more-liquid Treasuries. Government bonds are used in a wide variety of hedging as well as speculative trading strategies by institutional investors. Also, inflows of international funds typically are placed in government bonds, at least until they are allocated to other asset classes. Those factors lead to greater volatility in Treasury yields than comparable-maturity corporate bonds.'
rationales.B: Treasuries more vol.
rationales.C: Treasuries more volatile.

questions.curriculum_ref: LDI Risks — Treasury vs corporate vol

questions.stem: *Distressed assets and illiquidity risk* in contingent immunization *most likely*:

options.A: Contingent immunization carries no risk related to asset liquidity or distressed pricing.
options.B: manager may increase portfolio risk by using active management if surplus above threshold, but if losses reduce surplus to some minimum amount, positions need to be adjusted to revert to passive duration-matching portfolio of high-quality bonds.
options.C: Active positions in a contingent immunization strategy never need to be unwound.

rationales.A: Incorrect. If losses push the surplus to the trigger, positions — possibly distressed or illiquid — must be liquidated to lock in immunization.
rationales.B:
Full statement: manager may increase portfolio risk by using active management if surplus above threshold, but if losses reduce surplus to some minimum amount, positions need to be adjusted to revert to passive duration-matching portfolio of high-quality bonds; distressed assets that become hard to value, such as tranches of subprime mortgage-backed securities, also become illiquid during financial crises.

Correct. Per the reading: 'manager may increase portfolio risk by using active management. But if losses reduce the surplus to some minimum amount, the positions need to be adjusted to revert to a passive duration-matching fixed-income portfolio of high-quality bonds. Distressed assets that become hard to value, such as tranches of subprime mortgage-backed securities, also become illiquid during financial crises.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The forced unwind at the surplus floor is the strategy's defining risk; illiquid holdings make it costly.

questions.stem: *Fundamental choice in LDI* *most likely*:

options.A: LDI managers face no meaningful implementation choices once the liability profile is measured.
options.B: The only implementation route in LDI is physical asset allocation; derivatives play no role.
options.C: LDI manager has fundamental choice between managing interest rate risk with asset allocation and with derivatives overlays.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The fundamental implementation choice — asset allocation versus derivatives overlay — remains after measurement.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Derivatives overlays are one of the two principal routes; the choice between them is the point.
rationales.C:
Full statement: LDI manager has fundamental choice between managing interest rate risk with asset allocation and with derivatives overlays; choice depends on thorough evaluation of risk and return trade-offs; in some circumstances, derivatives might be deemed too expensive or risky particularly regarding available collateral and cash holdings; then manager might choose to increase holdings of long-term, high-quality bonds that have high duration statistics; growth of government zero-coupon bonds (US Treasury STRIPS) facilitates that asset reallocation process.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In summary, an LDI manager has a fundamental choice between managing interest rate risk with asset allocation and with derivatives overlays. As with all financial management decisions, the choice depends on a thorough evaluation of risk and return trade-offs. In some circumstances, derivatives might be deemed too expensive or risky, particularly regarding available collateral and cash holdings. Then the manager might choose to increase holdings of long-term, high-quality bonds that have high duration statistics. The growth of government zero-coupon bonds, such as US Treasury STRIPS (Separate Trading of Registered Interest and Principal of Securities), facilitates that asset reallocation process.'

questions.stem: *Callable bond swaption case study* — write receiver swaption to defease callable debt *most likely*:

options.A: when corporation issued callable bond, effectively bought call option giving flexibility to refinance at lower cost if benchmark rates and/or credit spread narrows.
options.B: No strategy exists for a corporation seeking to defease callable debt using swaptions.
options.C: The case study addresses callable debt but proposes only open-market repurchase as the remedy.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when corporation issued callable bond, effectively bought call option giving flexibility to refinance at lower cost if benchmark rates and/or credit spread narrows; writing receiver swaption 'sells' that call option and corporation captures value of embedded call option by means of premium received; if market rates in three years higher than strike rate on swaption and yield on debt security, both options expire out of the money; if market rates fall and bond prices go up, both options in the money — corporation sells 7-year bonds (assets) and uses proceeds to call debt liabilities at par value, gain offsets loss on closing swaption.

Correct. Per the case study solution: 'The derivatives consultant should recommend that the corporation choose the fourth option and write a receiver swaption — that is, an option that gives the swaption buyer the right to enter into a swap to receive fixed and pay floating. When the corporation issued the callable bond, it effectively bought the call option, giving the corporation the flexibility to refinance at a lower cost of borrowed funds if benchmark rates and/or the corporation's credit spread narrows. Writing the receiver swaption "sells" that call option, and the corporation captures the value of the embedded call option by means of the premium received. Suppose that market rates in three years are higher than the strike rate on the swaption and the yield on the debt security. Then both options — the embedded call option in the bond liability, as well as the swaption — expire out of the money. The asset and liability both have seven years until maturity and are non-callable. Suppose instead that market rates fall and bond prices go up. Both options are now in the money. The corporation sells the seven-year bonds (the assets) and uses the proceeds to call the debt liabilities at par value. The gain on that transaction offsets the loss on closing out the swaption with the counterparty.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Writing a receiver swaption monetizes the embedded call the issuer effectively owns.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The strategy presented is the swaption sale — the issuer bought refinancing flexibility with the call and can sell an offsetting option.

drills.los_text: discuss bond indexes and the challenges of managing a fixed-income portfolio to mimic the characteristics of a bond index

questions.stem: *Bond markets vs equity markets* size *most likely*:

options.A: Bond and equity markets are approximately the same size, with similar numbers of securities outstanding.
options.B: fixed-income markets are much larger and broader than equity markets, and number of fixed-income securities outstanding is vastly larger as reflected in broad market indexes.
options.C: The reading offers no comparison between fixed-income and equity market scale.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Fixed-income markets are MUCH larger and broader, with vastly more securities outstanding.
rationales.B:
Full statement: fixed-income markets are much larger and broader than equity markets, and number of fixed-income securities outstanding is vastly larger as reflected in broad market indexes; MSCI World Index (23 developed countries, 85% market cap) has ~1,600 securities; Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index (24 local currency markets) has more than 16,000 securities; those fixed-income issuers represent much wider range of borrowers than relatively narrow universe of companies issuing equity.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Fixed-income markets are much larger and broader than equity markets, and the number of fixed-income securities outstanding is vastly larger as reflected in broad market indexes. For instance, the MSCI World Index, capturing equities in 23 developed market countries and 85% of the available market capitalization in each market, consists of about 1,600 securities, whereas the Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index, covering global investment-grade debt from 24 local currency markets, consists of more than 16,000 securities. Those fixed-income issuers represent a much wider range of borrowers than the relatively narrow universe of companies issuing equity securities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The scale comparison is drawn explicitly — a single issuer can have dozens of bonds against one common stock.

questions.stem: *Bloomberg Barclays US Aggregate Index* *most likely* includes:

options.A: The Bloomberg Barclays US Aggregate contains no published constituent or sector data.
options.B: The US Aggregate covers a single sector: US Treasury securities only.
options.C: oldest and most widely recognized US bond market index (one of four regional aggregate benchmarks that constitute Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Constituent rules and sector weights are published and transparent.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The Aggregate spans Treasuries, agencies, corporates, and securitized sectors — one of four regional aggregates in the Global Aggregate.
rationales.C:
Full statement: oldest and most widely recognized US bond market index (one of four regional aggregate benchmarks that constitute Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index); includes US Treasuries, government agency securities, corporate bonds, mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities; although large number of index constituents provides means of risk diversification, in practice it is neither feasible nor cost-effective for investors to pursue full replication approach with broad fixed-income market index.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, the oldest and most widely recognized US bond market index, the Bloomberg Barclays US Aggregate Index (one of four regional aggregate benchmarks that constitute the Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index), includes US Treasuries, government agency securities, corporate bonds, mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. Although the large number of index constituents provides a means of risk diversification, in practice it is neither feasible nor cost-effective for investors to pursue a full replication approach with a broad fixed-income market index.'

questions.stem: *Debt vs equity securities* per issuer Exhibit 24 *most likely*:

options.A: as of October 2020, Royal Dutch Shell had 57 bonds outstanding across four currencies (some both fixed and floating rate), range of maturities under a year to bonds maturing in 2052.
options.B: Exhibit 24 provides no issuer-level data comparing debt and equity securities outstanding.
options.C: The reading makes no comparison of security counts between debt and equity for any issuer.

rationales.A:
Full statement: as of October 2020, Royal Dutch Shell had 57 bonds outstanding across four currencies (some both fixed and floating rate), range of maturities under a year to bonds maturing in 2052; BHP Billiton 22 bonds vs 1 common; Johnson & Johnson 37 vs 1; Ford Motor Company 104 fixed-income securities vs 2 common equity + 2 preferred equity; existence of many debt securities for a particular issuer suggests many near substitutes may exist for investor seeking to pursue enhanced index strategy.

Correct. Per Exhibit 24 (Debt and Equity Securities Outstanding for Select Issuers): 'Royal Dutch Shell PLC: 57 Fixed-Income, 1 Common Equity, 0 Preferred. BHP Billiton Limited: 22, 1, 0. Johnson & Johnson: 37, 1, 0. Ford Motor Company: 104, 2, 2.' And: 'As of October 2020, Royal Dutch Shell had 57 bonds outstanding across four currencies, some of which were both fixed and floating rate, with a range of maturities from under a year to bonds maturing in 2052. The existence of many debt securities for a particular issuer suggests that many near substitutes may exist for an investor seeking to pursue an enhanced index strategy.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exhibit details a real issuer's outstanding bonds by currency, coupon type, and maturity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Royal Dutch Shell's 57 bonds across four currencies against its common equity is the illustration.

questions.stem: *Fixed-income markets trading patterns* *most likely*:

options.A: Fixed-income trading is predominantly exchange-based with central limit order books like equities.
options.B: largely over-the-counter markets that rely on broker/dealers as principals to trade using quote-based execution process rather than order-based trading systems common in equity markets.
options.C: Bond and equity market structures are identical in venue and execution process.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Bond markets are largely OTC, quote-driven through broker/dealer principals — with Basel III raising dealers' inventory costs.
rationales.B:
Full statement: largely over-the-counter markets that rely on broker/dealers as principals to trade using quote-based execution process rather than order-based trading systems common in equity markets; rising cost of maintaining risk-weighted assets on dealer balance sheets because of Basel III capital requirements has had adverse effect on fixed-income trading and liquidity; broker/dealers reduced bond inventories; dealers less willing to support larger 'block' trades preferring smaller trade sizes; significant decline in proprietary trading among dealers has had greater pricing effect on less liquid 'off-the-run' bonds.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Unlike equity securities, which trade primarily over an exchange, fixed-income markets are largely over-the-counter markets that rely on broker/dealers as principals to trade in these securities using a quote-based execution process rather than the order-based trading systems common in equity markets. The rising cost of maintaining risk-weighted assets on dealer balance sheets because of Basel III capital requirements has had an adverse effect on fixed-income trading and liquidity. Broker/dealers have reduced bond inventories because of higher capital costs. With lower trading inventories, dealers have both a limited appetite to facilitate trading at narrow bid–offer spreads and are less willing to support larger "block" trades, preferring execution in smaller trade sizes. Finally, a significant decline in proprietary trading among dealers has had a greater pricing effect on less liquid or "off-the-run" bonds.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Quote-based OTC execution versus order-based exchange trading is the structural difference.

questions.stem: *US TRACE system* *most likely*:

options.A: No US system exists for reporting over-the-counter bond transactions.
options.B: US corporate bond trades are reported voluntarily, at each dealer's discretion.
options.C: US Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — regulators developed system that facilitates mandatory reporting of over-the-counter transactions in eligible fixed-income securities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. TRACE — the Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — is that system.
rationales.B: Incorrect. TRACE reporting is MANDATORY for covered OTC transactions.
rationales.C:
Full statement: US Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine — regulators developed system that facilitates mandatory reporting of over-the-counter transactions in eligible fixed-income securities; all broker/dealers that are FINRA (Financial Industry Regulatory Authority) member firms must report corporate bond transactions within 15 minutes of occurrence; vast majority of fixed-income securities either do not trade at all or trade only a few times during the year; average trade size in dollar terms in US investment-grade bond market is roughly 70 times size of average stock trade.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Fixed-income trading in many markets is difficult to track. In some markets, regulators developed systems that facilitate mandatory reporting of over-the-counter transactions in eligible fixed-income securities, such as the US Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine (TRACE) system. All broker/dealers that are Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) member firms must report corporate bond transactions within 15 minutes of occurrence. It is important to note the distinct nature of fixed-income trading versus equities. The vast majority of fixed-income securities either do not trade at all or trade only a few times during the year. Only a small fraction trade every business day, according to MarketAxess, a leading electronic trading provider. It is also important to note that the average trade size in dollar terms in the US investment-grade bond market is roughly 70 times the size of the average stock trade.'

questions.stem: *Matrix pricing* (evaluated pricing) *most likely*:

options.A: estimation process for fixed-income instruments that are not actively traded and therefore do not have observable price.
options.B: no concept.
options.C: no method.

rationales.A:
Full statement: estimation process for fixed-income instruments that are not actively traded and therefore do not have observable price; makes use of observable liquid benchmark yields (Treasuries of similar maturity and duration) as well as benchmark spreads of bonds with comparable times to maturity, credit quality, and sector/security type to estimate current market yield and price; asset managers typically outsource this function to global custodian or external vendor; estimation analysis is another potential source of variation between index performance and portfolio returns.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The illiquid nature of most fixed-income instruments gives rise to pricing and valuation challenges for asset managers.
